summary:
Guilá Naquitz Cave, located in the state of Oaxaca, Mexico, is one of the most important sites for understanding the origins of agriculture in the Americas. Excavated by Kent Flannery of the University of Michigan in the 1960s, the site provided botanical evidence of early squash, bean, and maize remains dating to between approximately 8000 and 6500 BCE, making it one of the earliest known sites with direct evidence of plant domestication in the Western Hemisphere.

The squash remains from Guilá Naquitz — later analyzed and radiocarbon dated more precisely — indicate cultivation of Cucurbita pepo (the species that includes modern pumpkins and acorn squash) as early as around 8000 BCE, representing some of the oldest known evidence of squash domestication anywhere in the world. The cave was used seasonally by small groups of foragers who moved between highland and lowland resource zones across the Oaxacan landscape, exploiting cactus fruits, acorns, maguey, and a range of wild game.

The archaeological sequence at Guilá Naquitz reveals a gradual transition from a pure foraging strategy toward increasing reliance on managed and eventually cultivated plants, without an abrupt transformation. This pattern of slow, incremental agricultural transition, sustained over millennia by groups who maintained a highly mobile lifestyle alongside incipient cultivation, represents a pattern now recognized as typical of agricultural origins in multiple world regions. The cave's evidence contributes importantly to understanding the early Mesoamerican agricultural tradition that eventually gave rise to the domestication of [[event:1439|maize]] and the development of Mesoamerican civilization.

read_more_links.title: Origins of Agriculture in the Americas - Wikipedia
